verify_notes: Mechanism verified in code exactly as described: member_delta (energy_registers.py:62-83) does 2x member_delta_pair, each _nx.window(tbl,[col]) = 2 endpoint queries (neuract.py:175-207), 4 q/member/paired key; window() already accepts a column list; fan-out is strictly sequential (panel_kwh loop members.py:199, per-binding delta bindings.py:177). Numbers re-derived from obs_db_queries/obs_stage_events: span 2a6684b057f9 = executor.card card 14, 52,683ms wall; 666 queries summing 51.4s, of which 617 single-column LIMIT-1 endpoint reads (308 ASC + 333 DESC, med 40ms, p90 194ms) totaling 50.4s — matches the claimed 616q/48.5s. Columns are exactly the paired registers (active import 244 / export 220, reactive 88+88) over 25 member tables. Fleet claim exact: 457 panel_aggregate executor.card spans, p50 6,080ms, median 109 queries. Re-measured batching live: 96 sequential single-col endpoint queries = 3.18s; paired-col = 1.58s (2x); ONE UNION ALL = ~1.00s with today's ::timestamptz-cast ORDER BY, but ~175ms only when ORDER BY uses the raw text column (the cast defeats the btree(timestamp_utc) index — EXPLAIN shows ~27ms parallel seq-scan+top-N sort per big-table leg; tables up to ~90k rows). So the claimed 165ms is real but requires the index-friendly ORDER BY (text order verified == cast order on sampled data; ISO fixed-offset) or an expression index — pure batching alone lands ~1s/statement. Constraints pass: generic data-layer fix (AI-first OK), CMD_V2 untouched, per-leaf honest-null preservable (UNION legs need per-table NULL padding since present columns differ per member), single-asset fill shares member_delta so values stay byte-identical (same row via same ORDER/LIMIT). Bonus amplifier found: data/neuract_pool.py holds ONE connection per DSN — all 4 concurrent cards serialize their ~1,626 queries through a single conn over a ~15.5ms-RTT tunnel, so batching also removes cross-card queue wait.

title: No memoization: identical member/delta reads re-executed per slot, per card, and per /api/frame
scenario: panel-overview page (4+ cards on the SAME panel); synced date-change frames
evidence: Card 14 slots 2 & 3 carry byte-identical element+range+role_filter -> _eval_elements (roster_eval.py:53-58) re-runs the full 28-member x 2-delta-key read (224 duplicate queries). Trace-wide: 1204 neuract queries but only 337 DISTINCT sql_texts; 867 re-executions carry 114.6s of the 141.6s recorded latency. Each of the 4 panel cards independently re-runs resolve() + rows() (28 latest reads, members.py:114-127) + panel_kwh (112 q, members.py:185-203). pairs_cache (roster_modes_agg.py:54-61) is per-slot only.
current_cost: ~867 duplicate query executions ~ 114.6s summed latency per panel page; frames repeat it all
proposal: TTLCache (30-60s, never-cache-empty — the codebase's existing pattern) keyed (table, colset, window) on member_delta_pair / members.rows / panel_kwh / bucketed_rolled; plus an in-run _roster_state memo for _eval_elements keyed (element-json, window, rf, ro). Pure data reads — AI decisions untouched.
est_saving: -8 to -15s wall on the 4-panel-card page (with EXEC-1); /api/frame date-change: frames 2..N become memo hits (~24s settle -> ~2-3s)
refined_saving: Corrected duplicate cost: 734 dup executions / 72.7s of 142.2s summed SQL on the cited slow-tunnel trace (auditor's 867/114.6s over-counted by keying on sql text without params); an identical-shape fast-tunnel run (sql_r_111f5975b2) shows 27.5s of 61.6s — per-query latency varies >2x with :5433 tunnel state. Standalone wall saving on the 4-panel-card page: slowest-card exec drops from ~45s toward ~20-25s on a slow-tunnel run (-15 to -25s wall) and roughly -5 to -10s on a fast run; the claimed -8 to -15s incremental (with EXEC-1) is defensible, arguably conservative on slow days. /api/frame date-change settle: the claim needs trimming — a date change gives NEW window params, so the dominant window-endpoint reads (81% of queries) MISS on the first frame; only frames 2..N (other cards, same new window, within TTL) become hits. Realistic settle ~24s -> ~5-12s, not 2-3s (2-3s holds only for same-window re-fetches). Biggest bang for smallest risk: memo (table, col-pair, window-params) on member_delta_pair/_nx.window first (666 of 734 dups), then members.rows/latest with a shorter TTL; single-flight optional (stampede measured at just 40 dups/8.5s).
risk_notes: Overlap warning: EXEC-1 targets the same 968 window-endpoint round-trips; if it batches/parallelizes per-member reads the memo's ABSOLUTE saving shrinks proportionally (the ~50-65% dedup ratio survives, applied to a smaller base) — do not sum the two findings' savings. Staleness: a 30-60s TTL on latest reads freezes live KPI values for the TTL (bounded, within meter cadence; precedent exists at 120s for resolution caches) — make it a knob, never-cache-empty, and consider window/delta-only memo for v1. Observability: memo hits emit no sql_trace/obs_db_queries records — payload_diff SQL-leg diffs and admin-console query counts will drop; replay tapes recorded with memo ON contain fewer reads (deterministic but shape-different). Concurrency: the 4 cards fill in parallel — without single-flight ~40 stampede dups (8.5s summed on the cited trace) survive; acceptable, but if /api/frame fan-out goes concurrent after a date pick, frames 1..N race the cold cache and the settle-improvement claim weakens further. Suite: the in-run _eval_elements memo must key on the selected pair-set identity (element-json + window + rf + ro + self), or slots differing only in reporting_only would cross-contaminate.
verify_notes: Mechanism verified line-by-line and the exact cited trace located (outputs/logs/sql_r_92be5bbca5.jsonl: 1204 neuract queries, 337 distinct sql_texts, 142.2s summed SQL; 4 panel cards, exec 26.6/32.0/34.5/45.0s = 138.1s, i.e. the fill is ~100% serial SQL round-trips, 4-way parallel, SQL wall-span 71s). All code claims hold TODAY: ems_exec/data/neuract.py caches only schema introspection + column_logged — latest/window/bucketed* hit SQL every call; energy_registers.member_delta on a paired register = 4 SQL/member (import+export pairs x window first/last), so panel_kwh over 28 load members = 112 q (members.py:185-203); card 14 recipe (cmd_catalog.card_fill_recipe) slots 1&2 are byte-identical entries slots (element {activeKwh,reactiveKvarh} deltas, range this-month, rf load) and _entries_slot re-runs _eval_elements per slot = 224 dup queries, arithmetic exact; pairs_cache (roster_modes_agg.py:54) is a per-slot local; prepare_ctx state is per-card ctx (roster.py:135-199) so each of the 4 cards re-runs resolve()+rows()+agg_row; /api/frame (host/server.py:289) is per-card fill_one_card. The finding UNDER-counts intra-card duplication: card 14 slot 0 (scalar) reads the same activeKwh deltas a THIRD time (+112 q), and panel_kwh is computed twice per card (agg_row roster.py:170 + lazy _context_vals roster_eval.py:79-85). ONE ARITHMETIC CORRECTION: the auditor deduped on sql TEXT only; the honest memo key is (sql, params) — window-endpoint reads with different range params are NOT dupes. Real numbers on the cited trace: 470 distinct (sql,params), 734 duplicate executions carrying 72.7s of the 142.2s summed (not 867/114.6s). Time-ordered memo simulation: paid 69.4s, saved 72.7s summed; in-flight stampede dups are only 40/8.5s, so a plain TTLCache without single-flight still captures 62.1s summed. Dup breakdown: window-endpoint 666 dups (the delta/pick_mover reads dominate: 976 of 1204 queries), latest 62, bucketed 6 — memoize member_delta_pair/window reads FIRST; a latest-read TTL is nearly optional and carries the staleness risk. No hard-constraint violation: pure data-plane memo (keys are table/colset/window — zero card knowledge, AI decisions untouched), matches the codebase's existing TTLCache + never-cache-empty precedent (data/ttl_cache.py, cache.resolution_ttl_s=120); per-leaf honesty preserved. Minor caveats: (a) 30-60s TTL on latest = bounded staleness on live KPI tiles — keep latest TTL short (10-30s) or skip latest initially; (b) memo hits stop emitting obs sql_trace records, so payload_diff SQL-leg counts / obs_db_queries rollups change (tooling, not a contract breach); (c) the in-run _eval_elements memo key must reflect the SELECTED pair set (rf+ro+self), which the proposal's key does.

title: Multi-asset compare fills assets sequentially — one full executor round per asset
scenario: 3-feeder compare (RESPONSE_MULTI p50 142s, slowest 410-441s)
evidence: host/multi_asset.py:127-138 `for group in groups: for asset in (group.get('assets')...): cards_i = assemble_cards(lane_i, asset, date_window)` — assemble_cards -> _run_cards full fan-out per asset, strictly serial. Per-asset exec parent span p50 1.1s / p90 7.7s, panel lanes far worse.
current_cost: 3x the per-asset exec time on every compare; with panel lanes that is 3 x ~15s = 45s of the compare tail
proposal: ThreadPool over assets (lanes are independent by construction: recipe authored once, rebind_consumer pure); effective only after EXEC-1 gives real DB concurrency.
est_saving: -15 to -30s p50 on a 3-asset panel compare exec stage (3x sequential -> max-of-3)
refined_saving: -2s p50 / -7s p90 / up to -31s worst-case on real compare traces (measured sum-minus-max of executor spans per trace, n=93) — NOT -15 to -30s p50. As a fraction of compare e2e: ~3-5% at p50 (48.5s), near-0% on the 400s+ tail. Best realizable cases: 2-panel compares -6 to -8s; a 3-panel compare could theoretically save 2x per-lane exec (~14-40s using panel-lane p50 7.2s/p90 20.6s) but such runs are rare in the observed data (most panel compares are 2-lane; most multi-lane compares are cheap feeder/DG classes at 1-2s/lane). Effort small, mechanism sound — worth doing, but reclassify from tail-fix to modest p50/p90 trim.
risk_notes: OVERLAPS: (1) EXEC-1 (executor DB concurrency) both enables and shrinks this — if EXEC-1 cuts per-asset exec time, EXEC-6's absolute saving falls below the numbers above; without EXEC-1, N-asset threads x 8 card-workers contend on the same :5433 tunnel and max-of-N will not be reached — cap total workers (e.g. shared pool across assets). (2) The much larger serial loop is run_pipeline_multi's per-CLASS lane loop (layer2_card_ai summed 43-83s for 2-class compares) and the asset_resolution stage (178-372s in tail traces) — if other findings cover those, EXEC-6's marginal contribution to compare e2e is small; do not double-count the compare tail. IMPLEMENTATION RISKS (low): obs executor spans must copy_context per asset thread (pattern exists at exec_cards.py:252) or spans detach from the trace; stage() jsonl appends from concurrent asset threads may interleave lines in pipeline_<rid>.jsonl (already true for the card pool); the exec wall-clock budget (_exec_budget_s, ER-8) is currently per-asset — parallel assets each get their own budget, which changes worst-case wall behavior from N*budget to 1*budget (desirable, but note it).
verify_notes:
MECHANISM VERIFIED, SAVING OVERSTATED ~5-10x AT MEDIAN.

Mechanism (all confirmed by reading code as of today):
- host/multi_asset.py:127-139: strictly serial `for group: for asset:` loop calling assemble_cards per asset. host/assemble.py:40 -> host/exec_cards.py _run_cards = full per-card ThreadPool fan-out (max_workers 8) PER ASSET, so cards parallelize within an asset but assets are sequential.
- Lanes are independent by construction: host/rebind_consumer.py deep-copies the class recipe per asset ("Returns a NEW l2 dict (deep-copied) so compare lanes never alias"); no shared mutable state between lane fills.
- Also confirmed: run/harness.py run_pipeline_multi:409-416 runs the per-CLASS run_pipeline lanes serially too (a separate, LARGER serial loop that includes LLM L2 authoring) — not covered by this finding.

Evidence numbers verified against obs_stage_events (cmd_catalog):
- Executor parent span (kind=stage, card_id null): n=558, p50=1121ms, p90=7684ms — matches claimed 1.1s/7.7s exactly.
- Panel pages (page_key ~ panel/pcc): p50=7.2s, p90=20.6s, max=45.0s — "panel lanes far worse" holds; claimed ~15s/lane sits between p50 and p90.
- RESPONSE_MULTI slowest: 410.6s / 415.5s / 441.7s — matches claimed 410-441s exactly.

Numbers REFUTED:
- "RESPONSE_MULTI p50 142s" does not reproduce: filtering pytest leakage (elapsed_ms>2s), real multi runs n=134 p50=48.5s (3-asset subset n=51 p50=44.6s, p90=212.9s). The raw p50 including test records is 7ms — 142s matches no cut I could construct.
- "45s of the compare tail" is wrong attribution: in the 6 slowest compare traces (582-758s total), asset_resolution consumed 178-372s and layer2_card_ai 22-83s, while the ENTIRE executor fill loop summed to 0.2-3.1s. The compare tail is asset-resolution + L2-authoring bound, not fill bound. EXEC-6 does approximately nothing for the 410-441s worst cases.
- Direct per-trace measurement of the actual saving (sum of executor spans minus max, i.e. serial->max-of-N ideal) across 93 real compare traces with >=2 executor spans: p50=2.1s, p90=6.8s, max=30.7s (a 5-lane DG compare, 66s total). Panel-section compares (pcc 1a/1b): saved 6-8s per trace against 77-256s totals.

Constraints: no violation. Host-only concurrency; single-asset path byte-untouched (multi_asset.py only reached at N>=2); AI layers untouched; per-leaf honesty preserved (fills already independent per card). The obs contextvars propagation pattern needed for asset threads already exists in-repo (exec_cards.py:252 contextvars.copy_context().run per card thread).

title: /api/frame date change re-runs the FULL member fan-out per card with zero reuse
scenario: date-control re-fetch on a panel-overview page (4 synced panel cards)
evidence: host/server.py:289-341 handle_frame -> fill_one_card, same seam as initial serve, no cross-frame state; FE posts one /api/frame per synced card (host/web/src/api.ts:150). Measured frame traces: n=262, p50 1.68s, p90 7.9s, max 19.6s PER CARD; 4 concurrent frames serialize on the single conn (EXEC-1) -> settle ~= sum ~= 24s worst.
current_cost: panel-page date change settles in ~10-24s; single frame p50 1.68s
proposal: EXEC-1 pool + EXEC-3 TTL memo keyed (mfm_id, window, colset) make frames 2..N nearly free; optional flag-gated /api/frames batch endpoint to cut N x HTTP + re-prepare.
est_saving: date-change settle on a 4-panel-card page ~24s -> ~2-3s; memo-hit frames <0.5s vs p50 1.68s
refined_saving: Combined stack (EXEC-1 pool + EXEC-3 memo + EXEC-4): 4-6-card panel-page date-pick settle 9-20s observed -> ~2-3s with a real neuract checkout pool alone (each frame at solo speed: ~100-130 queries x 17.5ms ~= 1.8-2.3s, run genuinely parallel); -> ~1-1.5s adding cross-frame single-flight memo (shared member latest-row read + shared rolled power series dedupe across the page's frames); repeat pick of an already-fetched historical window <0.5s (memo hit) vs p50 1.68s -- that sub-claim holds. HOWEVER, EXEC-4's OWN increment is only the ~1-1.5s first-pick dedupe + the <0.5s repeat-pick case: roughly 85% of the headline 24s->2-3s belongs to EXEC-1 (serialization removal). Do not book it twice. Post-fix bottleneck: WITHIN-frame serial query count (~2s floor at 130 queries x 17.5ms) -- going below ~2s on a first pick needs within-frame read parallelism or member-batched SQL (e.g. UNION ALL across member gic_* tables), neither of which is in this proposal.
risk_notes: (a) A plain TTL memo does NOT make the 4 simultaneous burst frames cheap -- they all miss at the same instant; single-flight/in-flight coalescing is required for the concurrent case. (b) Memo must be never-cache-empty (the 2026-07-09 member-cache-poison class) and keyed on the RESOLVED (start,end): 'today'/live windows have end=now so keys never repeat -- memo hits only benefit historical presets (yesterday, last-7-days), which is fine since that IS the date-change scenario, but quantizing 'now' to widen hits would trade up-to-TTL staleness on live data. (c) The neuract pool must keep the existing connect_timeout/keepalives + drop-on-flap semantics (tunnel :5433 self-heal) -- copy db_client.py's checkout pattern, not a naive pool. (d) Page-scale bursts observed at 16-20 frames x ~100 queries: after pooling, watch server-side contention on the remote archbox PG through the single SSH tunnel.
verify_notes: Mechanism verified end-to-end. (1) host/server.py:279-341 handle_frame -> fill_one_card is the same stateless seam as the initial serve; CmdCard.tsx:46-51 fires one /api/frame POST per synced history card on a shared-window change (fetchCardFrame in api.ts). (2) panel_aggregate.py re-runs the FULL member fan-out per frame (_members.rows over the fixed _MEMBER_COLS superset + per-column bucketed_rolled + per-member energy register pairs); the ONLY caches in the read path are schema/introspection and roster-resolution TTL -- no time-series result is ever reused across frames or cards. (3) Root cause of the serialization sharpened: data/neuract_pool.py holds ONE psycopg2 connection per DSN key (plain dict, no checkout pool) -- the multi-conn pool that went live 2026-07-12 (data/db_client.py) only serves the cmd_catalog q() door, NOT the neuract time-series door. (4) Numbers reproduced exactly from obs_traces: n=262, p50 1680ms, p90 7916ms, max 19602ms. New evidence beyond the claim: overlap-0 frames p50 1.26s vs 7+ overlapping frames p50 8-14s (monotone contention signal); per-query 17.5ms solo vs 145ms contended (~8x, same gic_* query shapes); slowest frame = 132 queries / 19.2s SQL out of 19.6s wall (98% SQL-bound); measured single-pick bursts of 4-6 frames settle 8.7-19.6s (claim of ~10-24s slightly generous at the top -- 19.6s was the observed max for such bursts). Frames carry 77-132 queries each (avg per latency bucket), confirming the fan-out is the cost. No constraint violations in the proposal: pool is infra-only, memo returns the same rows the read would (per-leaf honesty intact), batch endpoint is additive/flag-gated, CMD_V2 untouched.

title: bucketed() reads one column per query; a multi-column scan is measured FREE
stage: executor series fill (single-asset + roster series modes)
scenario: trend/history cards: 6-series single-asset card; 3-key x 28-member panel trend
evidence: neuract.py:282-304 bucketed(table, col,...) = one GROUP BY scan per column; series_fill._bucketed_values fires per FIELD; members.bucketed_multi (members.py:275-348) per key x per member; bucketed_rolled_members per member. Measured on :5433: 30d hourly 1-col scan 38ms vs SAME scan with 6 AVG columns 35ms. neuract.series() (:213-249) already implements the multi-column form.
current_cost: K series columns = K identical window scans per table; run_card median 35 q/card p50 816ms is round-trip bound
proposal: Group bucketed reads per (table, window, sampling) into one multi-column query in fill() and per member in bucketed_multi; pad absent columns None (honest-degrade preserved).
est_saving: -2.2s per 3-key 28-member trend card (84 -> 28 queries x ~40ms); -0.2s per 6-series single-asset card; multiplies with EXEC-1/3
refined_saving: Per observed 2-key 30-member panel trend card: 60->30 queries saves ~1.0-2.7s (half of measured 2.1-5.4s bucketed time); the claimed 3-key 28-member -2.2s is consistent (2/3 of a 3-key total). Median trend card (14 queries/3 tables/748ms): saves ~0.4-0.6s. 6-series single-asset card: -0.25 to -0.35s (5 eliminated queries x 52ms p50 + the _anchor_timestamps duplicate read reusing the merged result). Worst observed card (60q/14.0s bucketed) saves ~7-9s. Savings land on executor wall (p50 1.2s, panel cards 20-42s), not e2e p50 (LLM l2_emit ~20s/card dominates).
risk_notes: (1) Do NOT implement the merge by reusing neuract.series(): series() DROPS buckets whose every requested column is NULL (neuract.py:244-245) while bucketed() keeps None-valued points; the merged read must preserve bucketed() per-column semantics or single-asset payloads stop being byte-identical. (2) Group key must be (table, window, sampling-after-_coarsen_minute_for_span); fields with differing per-field sampling stay separate queries. (3) In bucketed_multi, per-spec `match` scopes members per key - the per-member merged query must include only the columns of specs matching that member; kind='event' (bucketed_edges, raw-row LAG shape) cannot join the merged GROUP BY and stays separate. (4) OVERLAP: composes as count x per-query-cost with EXEC-1/3 - savings do not add; if a ts expression index (ts_index_fn/ts_imm hook already exists in config) or fan-out parallelism lands first, per-query cost drops from ~52ms toward ~5-10ms and this fix's standalone saving shrinks to ~0.3-0.6s on the 60-query card. Claim both only as a combined residual, not a sum.
verify_notes: Mechanism verified line-by-line: neuract.bucketed (neuract.py:282-304) is one GROUP BY scan per column; series_fill._bucketed_values fires per field (fill.py:183) with _anchor_timestamps (fill.py:342,453) adding a duplicate axis read; members.bucketed_multi (members.py:275-348) loops specs x members serially; bucketed_rolled_members per member; zero parallelism in any fan-out path, so DB time = wall time. Multi-column-is-free reproduced live on :5433: 273k-row table 1-col 98-113ms vs 6-col AVG 102-107ms; median 53k-row table 1-col 59-84ms vs 3-col 62ms (seq scan + ::timestamptz text cast dominates; no ts index). Production cost verified from cmd_catalog.obs_db_queries: 6,843 single-col bucketed queries p50 52ms / avg 77ms / p95 226ms; of 1,027 executor cards, 379 fire bucketed reads (median 14 queries over 3 distinct tables = 748ms; p90 26q/2.6s); real panel-trend cards observed at 30 tables x 2 keys = 60 queries costing 2.1-5.4s bucketed DB time per card. Executor card DB total p50 3.3s over ~45 queries confirms round-trip-count bound. Constraints clean: generic data-layer grouping (AI-first ok), CMD_V2 untouched, per-column results byte-identical (same aggregates over the same row-driven bucket axis). Bonus: bucketed_delta (MAX-MIN, neuract.py:408-439) shares the same scan shape and can join the same merged query; only bucketed_edges (raw-row rising edge) cannot.

title: rows(): 28 sequential latest-row reads per panel card — one UNION ALL statement suffices
scenario: every panel_aggregate card (both recipe and recipe-less paths)
evidence: members.py:114-127 sequential loop of _nx.latest per member; obs card-14 span shows 24 x 22-column `ORDER BY ts_imm DESC LIMIT 1` reads totalling 1.8s contended. Measured UNION ALL 28-table endpoint batch = 165ms.
current_cost: ~0.7-1.8s per panel card just for member latest rows, repeated per card on the page (see EXEC-3)
proposal: Build one UNION ALL statement over the member tables (per-table SELECT lists from the shared present_columns cache), or parallelize the loop under EXEC-1.
est_saving: -1.5s per panel card before EXEC-1, -0.5s after; x4 cards/page
refined_saving: Per 28-32-member panel card: ~0.45-0.5s uncontended floor (0.5s -> 0.02s), 1.5-3.3s in real contended page runs (median ~2s observed, slightly above the claimed 1.8s). Per 10-member panel card: 0.1-0.5s. Per page at 3-7 panel-aggregate passes: ~1.5-2s uncontended, 4-10s+ contended, before EXEC-1. After EXEC-1 parallelization the residual is ~0.2-0.5s/card (pool-wave round trips vs one statement) — the claimed -0.5s post-EXEC-1 holds. OVERLAP: EXEC-3 (cross-card member-row reuse) covers the same reads — the per-run signatures are 19/21/28/29-col variants over the SAME 32 tables, so a superset cache absorbs most of EXEC-7's per-page value; count the two findings once, not additively. Also note EXEC-7 does not shift e2e p50 by itself: l2_emit LLM (~20s/card) and the jsonb_each fullness-probe whale (13-27s per query, 549s of one 738s run) dominate the runs where they appear.
risk_notes: Two implementation cautions, neither a blocker: (1) per-leaf honesty — an empty table's UNION ALL arm returns no row (my live test: 32 arms -> 20 rows), so the implementation must re-pad absent members to {} and per-table-absent columns to None (per-arm NULL placeholders over the shared column superset), preserving the current honest-null contract; (2) replay/hooks tapes key on SQL text (sql.nx), so previously recorded replay tapes and any pinned-replay tests will miss against the new statement shape — regenerate tapes or key the seam tolerant. Statement size is fine (32 arms x ~29 cols measured OK, well under limits). No AI-first, CMD_V2, or single-asset byte-contract impact: single-member calls (roster.py:167) can keep the existing one-table path byte-identical.
verify_notes: Mechanism verified in code: ems_exec/executor/members.py:114-127 rows() sequentially calls _nx.latest() per member; each is one 'SELECT <cols> FROM neuract.<gic_*> ORDER BY neuract.ts_imm(ts) DESC LIMIT 1' round trip (ems_exec/data/neuract.py:137-151); no batching or parallelism exists in ems_exec. Called per card via executor/roster.py:157 and renderers/panel_aggregate.py:218. Cost re-derived from obs SQL traces (outputs/logs/sql_r_*.jsonl): large panels run 21-29-col latest passes over 32-35 member tables at 1.5-3.3s summed per pass (e.g. sql_r_414855fc2d: 28 cols x 32 tables x 7 passes ~2.75s each; sql_r_279c13e84b ~3.3s/pass), 3-7 passes per run; 10-member panels ~0.25-0.6s/pass. The cited card-14 numbers (24 x 22-col, 1.8s, ts_imm) sit inside this observed distribution. Live uncontended measurement on the same DB: 32 sequential latest reads = 473-498ms (~15ms/read, round-trip bound); one UNION ALL over the same 32 tables = 20-21ms warm or cold — the claimed 165ms batch is conservative. Traces show 25-65ms/read under load vs 15ms idle, i.e. contention inflates each of the 28-32 round trips, so the contended saving exceeds the uncontended floor exactly as claimed.

title: Event KPIs: sequential LAG raw-row scans per member per flag column; full-table when window is open
stage: executor event fill (edge_count / bucketed_edges / bucketed_multi kind=event)
scenario: event-timeline panel card over 28 members; snapshot event KPIs (no window)
evidence: neuract.py:347-405 LAG over raw rows; members.py:313-317 sequential member loop. Measured: 163ms full-table LAG on a 273k-row table, 83ms with a 7d window pushed down. A windowless snapshot event count = full-table scan per member.
current_cost: 28 x 83-163ms ~ 2.3-4.6s per event card, serialized
proposal: Batch per (col, window) across members via UNION ALL of per-table LAG subqueries; parallelize under EXEC-1; consider a start-bound for snapshot counts only with owner sign-off (lifetime counts are a contract).
est_saving: -2 to -4s per event-timeline panel card (28 scans -> 1 batched statement ~0.3s)

title: _context_vals recomputes panel_kwh (~112 queries) though agg_row already holds the identical value
scenario: panel cards whose reducers/sankey reference the panel context (panel_kwh)
evidence: roster_eval.py:76-85 `_context_vals` calls _members.panel_kwh(state['pairs'], state['window'], energy_col) — a full 28-member x 4-query recompute — while prepare_ctx already stored the same number in agg_row[energy_col] (roster.py:169-177).
current_cost: ~112 queries x ~40ms ~ 4.5s when triggered
proposal: Read state['agg_row'].get(energy_col) when present; fall back to the recompute only if absent.
est_saving: -4.5s on context-referencing panel cards (sankey/reducer class)
refined_saving: Removes exactly ONE duplicate panel_kwh per roster card that has any aggregates/scalar/entries/group_agg/sankey slot: measured -1.65s/card on a 28-member panel and -0.44s/card on an 8-member panel at quiet-time DB latency (~15ms/query); scales to roughly -5s to -12s/card under live-sweep DB contention (95-111ms/query observed). Claimed -4.5s is a reasonable contended mid-range for the largest panel; use -0.4s to -1.7s/card as the honest quiet-time figure, times the number of triggered cards on a panel page (an 8-card panel-overview page triggers it on most cards). Also removes 32-112 round-trips/card of :5433 tunnel pressure, which co-benefits concurrent queries.
risk_notes: (1) Fall back on ABSENT KEY, not on None value: agg_row always carries the energy_col key when the roster roll-up was injected, and a None there means the recompute would also yield None -- falling back on None just wastes the queries in the honest-null case but is harmless. (2) Guard/comment the caller-provided _agg_row case: unreachable today (legacy panel_aggregate ctx has no mfm_id so no roster state), but if a future path injects a supply-side agg_row alongside a roster recipe, the context panel_kwh would follow the injected side -- which is arguably correct since panel_kw already does exactly that. (3) OVERLAP: does not stack with any other finding that caches/batches neuract window() reads, pools member_delta, or caches panel_kwh -- same queries. Also note prepare_ctx's own panel_kwh (the legitimate copy) remains; this fix removes only the duplicate half of the panel_kwh share of executor_core's ~20s/run neuract_ts time. (4) Not an e2e-p50 mover: l2_emit (19.8s/card p50) stays the bottleneck; this trims executor fill wall time on panel pages only. Suite-green expected: no test asserts the _context_vals call pattern.
verify_notes: Mechanism verified line-for-line: roster_eval.py:76-85 _context_vals recomputes panel_kwh(pairs, window, energy_col) while roster.py:169-177 prepare_ctx already stored the identical value via members.agg_row (members.py:181 sets row[energy_col]=panel_kwh with the SAME pairs/window/energy_col/role_filter="load" defaults on both sides). Query arithmetic confirmed: 4 SQL per load-side member (pick_mover reads import+export registers, each via neuract.window() = ASC+DESC LIMIT-1 pair); PCC Panel-4 (mfm 320) has exactly 28 load-side members = 112 queries. Scope is BROADER than claimed: _context_vals(state) is evaluated eagerly as the context= argument in every roster_modes_agg reduce (lines 40/43/101/147) and unconditionally in roster_modes_sankey (126/226), so ANY roster card with an aggregates/scalar/entries/group_agg/sankey slot pays the recompute once per card run, not just cards that reference panel_kwh. Live measurement: 1.65s per recompute on the 28-member panel (quiet, ~15ms/query, warm==cold so not cache-absorbed), 0.44s on an 8-member panel; profiler live-sweep executor_core neuract_ts queries ran median 95ms/mean 111ms under contention, putting the contended cost of the same 112 queries at ~10-12s. The claimed 4.5s (40ms/query) is a fair mid-range. No constraint violation: generic plumbing, honest-null preserved (agg_row energy value None iff recompute would be None on same inputs), leaf-meter path byte-identical (no injected _agg_row -> key absent -> fallback recompute), no test pins the recompute. The only divergence path (legacy panel_aggregate injecting a supply-side _agg_row for incomer scope) is unreachable: that ctx carries no mfm_id so prepare_ctx never builds roster state. Bonus correctness: today the two reads happen seconds apart and can disagree if rows land between them (context panel_kwh vs the panel Sigma leaf); reusing agg_row removes that self-contradiction race, consistent with panel_kw which ALREADY reads agg_row.

title: freshness.apply queries latest_ts once per card — identical across all cards on one table
scenario: 8-card single-asset dashboard; every /api/frame
evidence: freshness.py:127 `_nx.latest_ts(asset_table)` per fill; card-14 span shows 3 single-col DESC LIMIT 1 reads (~0.4s contended); an 8-card page on one table repeats the identical query 8x.
current_cost: (N-1) x 30-150ms per page, worse under contention
proposal: TTLCache(10-30s) keyed table inside neuract.latest_ts (staleness far below the freshness thresholds it feeds).
est_saving: -0.2 to -1s per page and per frame burst
refined_saving: Executor freshness/backfill leg (the finding as stated): p50 ~0s, p90 ~0.5s, max ~2.7s per page-run — the claimed -0.2 to -1s is the p75-p95 band of RTM/multi-asset pages, not the median page (most pages have 0-2 such queries). Same per /api/frame date-sync burst on RTM cards. The SAME fix additionally saves ~4.6s (p50) to ~12-14s (p90/max) of duplicate query time per panel-aggregate run at L2 emit (128->32 queries; wall-clock saving ~5-10s since the member-block DB leg sits on the critical path before each card's LLM call, but the 4-way-concurrent first-miss still pays ~32 queries ~5s). Net honest estimate: -0-0.5s median page, -0.5-2.7s RTM multi-asset pages, -5-10s wall on panel-aggregate pages. Post-fix, l2_emit remains LLM-dominated (19.8s/card baseline), so page e2e p50 barely moves; panel-aggregate p90 improves materially.
risk_notes: 1) MUST follow never-cache-empty (v48 member-cache-poison lesson): never cache a None latest_ts, or a tunnel flap pins Offline badges for the TTL. 2) Concurrent-miss: L2 emit runs 4 cards in parallel and their member-block loops start simultaneously — without single-flight coalescing the burst-front misses still duplicate; simple TTLCache gets ~60-75% of the L2 dedup, a per-table lock gets ~100%. 3) Staleness is safe: <=30s-old REAL timestamp vs the 180s stale_after_s threshold; only the lastUpdateLabel HH:MM:SS can lag by <=TTL and window_backfill anchors shift <=TTL on 30-day windows — no fabrication, per-leaf honesty intact. 4) Place the cache INSIDE neuract.latest_ts (tests monkeypatch via the newest= override and module attr; keep the cache clearable like _ROWS_CACHE for test isolation). 5) No constraint violations: no per-card hardcode, CMD_V2 untouched, byte-identical single-asset contract unaffected beyond accepted TTL staleness.
verify_notes: Mechanism verified: freshness.py:127 calls _nx.latest_ts(asset_table) per card fill and neuract.latest_ts (ems_exec/data/neuract.py:157) has NO cache — despite the module already importing TTLCache for _LOGGED_CACHE, and fab_guards/class23_source.py:19 documenting 'latest_ts is itself cached upstream' which is FALSE today (the cache was assumed but never built — strong supporting evidence). One correction to the stated mechanism: freshness.apply pre-scans the payload (freshness.py:108-124) and only hits the DB when a BLANK freshness contract exists — i.e. RTM-class cards (36/37/38), not every card on the page, so '8-card page repeats 8x' overstates the single-page case. latest_ts is also called uncached from series_router.py:156 (backfill anchor), fab_guards class23 (own per-process cache, fine), and layer2/emit/panel_members_block.py:39 (per member per card at L2 emit). Numbers re-derived from 573 full-run obs traces (outputs/logs/trace_*.jsonl): latest_ts-shaped query latency p50 82ms / p90 187ms / max 1808ms (n=5740). Executor-stage same-table duplicates confirmed in 57/118 runs (cards 36/37/38+40/41/71 re-reading the same table up to 8x, e.g. trace_t_17819d89: wasted 2693ms). /api/frame claim verified: handle_frame → fill_one_card → fill → freshness.apply, so a date-sync frame burst re-fires the identical query per linked card. UNDERSOLD UPSIDE: the same one-line fix kills the far larger layer2_card_ai duplication — panel-aggregate runs fire 128 latest_ts queries (4 cards x the SAME 32 member tables, concurrent over a 15.1s wall window that precedes each card's LLM call): wasted duplicate query time p50 4.6s / p90 12.3s / max 14.1s per run, present in 65/177 runs with these queries. If the audit carries a separate L2 members-block finding, the savings OVERLAP — one TTLCache in neuract.latest_ts covers both; do not double-count.

title: _field_value re-queries latest() per field when the batched latest row holds NULL for that column
scenario: single-asset cards on partially-dark meters (NULL-ish columns)
evidence: fill.py:203-205 `raw = (latest_row or {}).get(col); if raw is None: raw = _nx.latest(asset_table,[col])` — the column was already in the batched latest read (fill.py:257-259), so the re-read provably returns the same NULL; one extra query per null raw field (~25-150ms each).
current_cost: 1-5 wasted queries per card on dark meters
proposal: Fall back only when `col not in latest_row` (not in the batched read), never on a NULL value — byte-identical results.
est_saving: -0.1 to -0.5s per card on dark meters
refined_saving: Per affected card: ~0.07-0.4s typical (1-5 re-reads x 74-210ms, serial inside the card's fill loop), up to 1-3s wall on heavy dark-meter cards. Per run: p50 0.94s / p90 4.3s summed across cards, partially masked wall-clock by the 8-worker card pool (host/exec_cards.py); ~31% of runs affected; fleet mean ~0.6s/run (236s over 402 runs). Executor-stage-relative this is significant; e2e-relative it is small next to l2_emit (~19.8s/card).
risk_notes: Low, with two watch-items. (1) Flap self-healing regression: when a :5433 tunnel flap fails the batched read, latest() returns all-None WITH keys present; today each field's re-read retries (pool reconnects) and can rescue the card mid-fill — with the fix the whole card's raw scalars honest-blank for that run (next run heals). Given the repo's member-cache-poison history, consider a one-shot batch retry when the batched read errored (latest() cannot currently distinguish error from empty table — both return all-None). (2) Keep the fallback for col-not-in-latest_row: it is load-bearing for derived-without-fn / unknown-kind fields excluded from raw_cols; removing it would change results. Overlap warnings: does NOT overlap the ts_imm index fix (already deployed); DOES fully overlap any finding proposing per-run latest() caching or cross-card latest dedup; a separate uncovered waste pool of repeated latest_ts/ts_imm anchor+freshness reads (~831 single ts reads in the same window, up to 300ms each) is NOT fixed by this and should not be double-credited.
verify_notes: Mechanism verified byte-exact: fill.py:254-259 batches every raw-field column into one _nx.latest(asset_table, raw_cols); fill.py:203-205 re-queries _nx.latest(asset_table,[col]) whenever the batched value is None. neuract.latest() (data/neuract.py:137-151) reads the SINGLE newest row (ORDER BY ts DESC LIMIT 1, no NULL-skipping, no cache), so the re-read provably returns the same NULL (only a sub-second new-row race differs). Cost re-derived from 402 last-48h sql_r_*.jsonl traces: 2,099 wasted re-reads matching the fill:205 signature, 236s total, p50 74ms / p90 210ms / p99 ~470ms per query; 123/402 runs affected (31%), per-affected-run waste p50 0.94s / p90 4.3s / max 28.8s; dominant column active_power_total_kw (NULL in newest row is common on gic_* meters that write column-groups on alternating rows). IMPORTANT: the neuract.ts_index_fn=ts_imm knob is ALREADY live (240 expression indexes exist; warm isolated query ~15ms) yet in-run cost is still 74ms p50 — the residual is tunnel RTT + run contention, so the R3 index fix does NOT overlap this saving; only not issuing the query removes it. Constraints: clean — generic executor mechanics (AI-first ok), honest-blank semantics unchanged, CMD_V2 untouched, and 'col not in latest_row' correctly preserves the load-bearing fallback for derived-without-fn/unknown-kind fields whose columns were never batched (kind not in ('raw','')) — do NOT change that leg. Two corrections to the finding: (a) effort is a ~2-line code edit in fill.py, not 'config'; (b) claimed per-query cost 25-150ms brackets the truth (74ms p50, 210ms p90) and claimed 1-5 queries/card is right for the median but understates heavy dark-meter cards (per-run max 82 re-reads).

title: Recipe-less panel renderer rolls the same member power series twice (bucketed series + load factor)
scenario: recipe-less panel cards (1/4/84/94/95) with a load-factor leaf
evidence: renderers/panel_aggregate.py:232 _fill_bucketed_series rolls declared series across members, then :236/:261 _fill_rolled_load_factor calls _rolled_series(member_rows, _KW, ...) again with no reuse even when active_power_total_kw was just rolled.
current_cost: one duplicated 28-member roll ~ 28 x 38ms ~ 1.1s per such card
proposal: Per-render memo of rolled series keyed (col, window, sampling, role_filter).
est_saving: -1.1s per recipe-less panel card with a load-factor leaf
refined_saving: ~0s in current production (path unreachable: non-routable pages + no card_payloads skeleton + valve 'on' sends all live panel cards through the roster interpreter). Latent value only: if the interpreter valve were flipped off, trend cards (7/10/11) falling back here with both a bucketed hourly kw field and an LF leaf would save ~88-137ms x load-side member count per card (~2.5-3.8s on a 28-member panel) from the proposed memo.
risk_notes: The proposed per-render memo keyed (col,window,sampling,role_filter) is itself safe (generic code, no per-card hardcode, byte-identical output, no honesty change) — but it fixes dormant code and would show 0ms in any live measurement, risking a false 'optimization applied' claim in the audit tally. If pursued anyway as valve-off insurance, keep the memo per-render-call only (no cross-request cache — member_rows freshness and the never-cache-empty lesson from the 2026-07-09 cache-poison incident). The real, measurable duplicate-roll savings are on the executor.card path (cards 36/40 repeated identical bucketed kw SQL within one trace) and would overlap any broader SQL-dedupe fix — attribute savings there, not here.
verify_notes: Code reading is accurate: ems_exec/renderers/panel_aggregate.py:232 (_fill_bucketed_series) and :236/:261 (_fill_rolled_load_factor -> _rolled_series(member_rows,_KW,'hourly')) share no memo, and neuract.bucketed has no result cache, so a dual roll WOULD issue duplicate per-member SQL. But the claimed cost is illusory because the trigger is unreachable in production today: (a) the recipe-less panel_aggregate set is exactly cards {1,4,84,94,95} (all other panel cards have card_fill_recipe rows and take the roster interpreter under roster.interpreter_enabled=on, never touching this code); (b) none of those five cards' pages is in routable_pages (overview-sld-3d, source-generation, and the transformer/source pages are absent from the 18 routable pages) so the router can never serve them; (c) none of the five has a card_payloads skeleton, so _card_payload() returns None and render() bails at line 207 before any roll; (d) all five are scalar KPI/driver/flow cards with no trend arrays — even with an LF leaf, _fill_bucketed_series would be a no-op and the LF roll would be the ONLY kw roll (necessary work, not duplication); (e) obs evidence: 2 days / 595 requests / 1,909 executor.card spans contain ZERO spans for these cards and ZERO renderer-path bucketed kw queries. Also the unit cost is wrong: real bucketed kw queries measure p50 88ms / avg 137ms (n=2,721 in obs_db_queries), not 38ms — a hypothetical 28-member duplicate would cost ~2.5-3.8s, but at observed frequency zero. Note: REAL within-trace duplicate bucketed-kw SQL exists on cards 36/40 (executor.card path, identical statements repeated 5-8x per trace, e.g. t_23706eb7: 8x two statements ~0.4s each) — that is a different mechanism/finding worth pursuing instead; the generic path's load_factor_fill.py:274 already memoizes, only this dormant legacy renderer lacks one.

title: Exec budget (45s) fires on queue time, not card work — spurious honest-blanks trigger user re-runs
scenario: panel-overview pages under the single-conn serialization
evidence: exec_cards.py:242-284 budget measured on wall while queries queue on the one conn (EXEC-1); trace t_10ca...: executor span exactly 45001ms, card 14 only rescued by the done-check race at 48.4s. Cards that need ~5s of real work blank as 'executor budget exceeded'.
current_cost: honest-blank cards + full user re-run (another ~40-140s) on heavy pages
proposal: No contract change needed: EXEC-1/2/3 bring wall under budget; optionally scale the budget on outstanding-query depth as a stopgap.
est_saving: eliminates the re-run tail (one avoided re-run = -40 to -140s perceived)
refined_saving: Standalone saving is small and mostly NOT latency: converting the 3-per-182-prompt (1.6%; 4.5% of panel prompts) budget blanks into completed cards costs +3..15s extra wall on those pages (running to ~48-60s instead of blanking at 45s) and removes a data-loss defect — a fully-fetched payload discarded 3.4s past deadline in the verified trace. The claimed -40..-140s 'avoided re-run' is behavioral speculation: no re-run telemetry exists, and a re-run of the same prompt hits the same structural single-conn serialization (warm schema caches shave little — 651/653 of card 14's queries were data reads, not probes), so the blank likely repeats and the re-run buys nothing. Amortized expected saving if users always re-run once: ~1.6% x 80s (measured panel-page run, r_92be5bbca5 elapsed 79.5s) = ~1-2s/prompt. OVERLAP: ~100% subsumed by EXEC-1/2/3 — with parallel neuract connections the t_10ca fan-out wall drops to ~max own-work (~16-20s), far under 45s, and EXEC-13's cost vanishes with zero config change. Treat EXEC-13 as a correctness/UX symptom of EXEC-1 and a cheap stopgap ONLY if EXEC-1 is deferred; do not count its saving additively with EXEC-1.
risk_notes: Low risk as proposed. Watchpoints: (a) raising/scaling the budget INCREASES page wall on affected pages (users wait for stragglers instead of getting a fast partial page) — the 45s cap is also the only bound on a black-holed :5433 read, so any depth-scaled budget needs a hard ceiling; (b) tests/test_regress_exec_budget.py pins current budget semantics — keep green; (c) a cheaper zero-risk companion fix visible in the verified trace: the finally-sweep discards a future that completes between timeout and sweep only if not fut.done() at sweep instant — card 14's data was already 93% fetched; a short grace re-check (e.g. wait up to a few s for near-done futures) would have shipped it, but that is a code change, not config; (d) all 3 occurrences were under sweep load on one morning — n=3 is too small to project a stable fleet rate; live-user rate may be lower (sweeps run hotter than users).
verify_notes: Mechanism verified against code + live telemetry. (1) host/exec_cards.py:242-254: one wall deadline (cfg ems_exec.card_budget_s=45, confirmed live in app_config) over the WHOLE fan-out via as_completed(timeout=...); finally-sweep (266-283) stamps not-done cards 'executor budget exceeded'. (2) The EXEC-1 dependency is real: data/neuract_pool.py keeps exactly ONE psycopg2 connection per DSN key (_POOL[key] = a single conn; _LOCK only guards dict access), so all executor threads' neuract time-series reads serialize on it; ems_exec/data/neuract.py:_run_raw times t0 before run_read, so recorded per-query latency includes wait-for-conn. (3) Cited trace t_10ca417759b44007b72f9e900e1cc2dd re-derived from obs_stage_events/obs_db_queries: executor span exactly 45001ms status=degraded; 4 cards recorded 139.0s of neuract query latency inside a 48.4s wall (2.9x oversubscription = queueing); card 14 ran 653 queries (47.3s recorded) but its TRUE execute share is ~16s (47.3/139 x 48.4s) — own work well under budget, queue pushed it over. EVIDENCE CORRECTION (strengthens the finding): card 14 was NOT rescued by the done-check race — executor outputs show completed:3 failed:1 and the legacy.exec record for card 14 is ok:false why='executor budget exceeded' at 45.0s; its fully-fetched payload (done 48.4s) was DISCARDED, and the response shipped at 08:56:04.7 with the card honest-blank. Also the '~5s of real work' figure is understated (~16s here), but still under budget so the point stands. FREQUENCY: 3 real occurrences ever (a 4th grep hit is pytest leakage, run_id t_fanout_stage/card 60) — all panel prompts (panel-2a, pcc-panel-04, panel-2b), all card 14, all 2026-07-12 08:44-08:56 under campaign2_volume3 sweep load: 3/182 volume3 cases (1.6%), 3/66 panel prompts (4.5%), 3/489 executor spans fleet-wide (0.6%). Volumes 1+2 (1500 cases) show ZERO because the budget was dead code until 2026-07-12 (exec_cards.py:243-249 comment: old as_completed form never fired). Executor wall p99=36s, so heavy panel pages run close to the 45s edge. CONSTRAINTS: proposal is clean — config-level, no per-card hardcode, per-leaf honesty preserved (budget blank is already an honest fetch-reason), no CMD_V2 writes; tests/test_regress_exec_budget.py pins budget semantics and must stay green if a depth-scaled budget stopgap lands.

title: Dead end (measured): post-fill CPU pass chain is negligible — do not fuse passes
scenario: all cards

Also: present_columns/column_logged already TTL-cached (120s); ts_imm indexes present (673 indexes); cmd_catalog reads avg 0.7ms.
current_cost: ~20-40ms CPU per card total
proposal: None — documented so no one spends effort here; per-leaf degradation chain stays as is.
est_saving: 0 (negative finding, prevents wasted optimization effort)
refined_saving: 0 confirmed (negative finding). Ceiling if anyone fused all CPU passes anyway: ~15-25ms/card on the largest payloads, ~5-10ms typical — 0.1-0.2% of layer2_card median, unmeasurable at e2e p50 37.8s.
risk_notes: Scope guard: this verdict covers the CPU passes ONLY. The same post-fill chain contains DB-backed passes (freshness -> neuract latest_ts probe; fab_guards CLASS-2 column_logged probes, TTL-cached 120s; scalar_mean_fill / scalar_tile_fill / load_factor_fill -> per-leaf window reductions = real neuract queries) which live in the executor's DB budget and can cost seconds per card on cold cache or tunnel flaps — do not read EXEC-14 as 'post-fill is free'; those DB legs overlap other executor-fill findings and stay optimizable. Minor: _null_untouched_placeholders is the heaviest CPU pass (7ms on 95KB) — still not worth touching. The ts_imm '673 indexes' side-claim is imprecise as written (240 in the default DB; count likely spans multiple db_links) but does not affect the verdict.
verify_notes: Mechanism verified: fill() (ems_exec/executor/fill.py:397-619) runs ~15 sequential fail-open post-fill passes + _sweep_sankeys in serve/run.py:125, with ONE deepcopy at entry (line 231). Independent re-bench on the real 95KB payload (card 20, outputs/prim_corpus/panel-overview-shell_voltage-current.json) reproduces the claimed per-pass numbers within 0.1-0.3ms: yscale 3.4ms (claimed 3.3), view_select 2.6 (2.5), display 2.0 (2.0), trend_badge 2.4 (2.4), sweep_sankeys 2.0 (1.9); 11 CPU passes sum to ~31ms on the LARGEST payload in the corpus, so 20-40ms/card is a correct upper band (typical 2-30KB cards: ~5-15ms). Against profiler ground truth (outputs/latency/summary.json): executor_card live median 1.19s / avg 6.0s is DB-dominated (FINDINGS.md: neuract_ts 239s over 2,153 pooled queries), so the CPU chain is ~1-3% of the executor stage and ~0.1-0.2% of layer2_card median (19.8s). Corroborating claims: present_columns/column_logged TTL cache verified (lib/ttl_cache via data/neuract_pool, never-cache-empty); ts_imm expression indexes verified present — 240 in the default pooled neuract DB via indexdef LIKE '%ts_imm%' (the claimed 673 likely sums across all neuract db_links; the finding's own name-pattern would return 0 — indexes are named *_tsimm and reference ts_imm() in indexdef); 'cmd_catalog 0.7ms' not reproduced exactly (live database-stage median 3.7ms) but non-load-bearing. Proposal is a no-op so no constraint (AI-first, per-leaf honesty, CMD_V2 read-only, byte-identity, suite) can be violated. Negative finding stands: fusing passes would recover at most ~15-25ms/card — do not spend effort here.

title: value_probe ORDER BY ::timestamptz defeats both live indexes -> 20-30s seq-scan chunks (the 1b p95-200s tail)
evidence: obs_db_queries 2d: 4,107 jsonb_each fullness probes avg 20-28s/40-table chunk, max 104.6s, 46,483s total DB wall; recent trace t_2aed84a5 spent 184.3s in 9 un-staged probe chunks + 3.9s single-table probe in asset_resolution. EXPLAIN ANALYZE on gic_27_n2_600_kva_ups_02_tm (255k rows): current cast shape 1,137.6ms Parallel Seq Scan (10,397 buffers) vs ts_imm expression 0.064ms Index Scan (13 buffers). Knob neuract.ts_index_fn='ts_imm' IS set and the executor door honors it, but value_probe builds ORDER BY from raw DATA_TS_CAST which matches neither btree(timestamp_utc) (295/302 tables) nor ts_imm expression index (240/302).
current_cost: ~25s per 40-table chunk; 184s per full-registry panel roster; 3.9s per single-table has_renderable_data probe; cache-miss vs cache-hit twin traces differ by ~205s
scenario: cold 5-8-card panel-overview page / any prompt after 120s cache TTL
proposal: Build the probe ORDER BY with the same knob-aware expression the executor uses (neuract.ts_imm(timestamp_utc::text) DESC), or ORDER BY raw text timestamp_utc DESC (plain _ts index covers 295/302 and ISO-8601 text sorts chronologically). ~3-line change in value_probe.py reusing config/neuract_dsn.ts_index_fn().
est_saving: 40 tables x 1,137ms seq scan -> 40 x ~0.1ms + 16ms RTT: chunk 25s -> <0.1s. Panel page with 9 chunks: -184s; typical cold single-asset run (1 chunk + 2 single-table probes): -25..-35s; removes the 1b live p95 200s tail
refined_saving: Slightly less than claimed at the floor, more at the fleet level. Per-chunk: 20-28s -> 0.1-1s (NOT <0.1s universally): 62/302 gic tables lack the ts_imm expression index (215k est rows combined -> ~1-2s residual seq-scan spread across a full-registry pass) and aux_hsd_plc_feedbacks (172k rows) has NO timestamp index at all (~0.4-0.7s residual whenever probed). Full-registry panel roster: 184s -> ~2-5s. Cold single-asset run: -20..-30s. Fleet: removes ~46,000s DB wall per 2 days (86% of all pipeline DB wall); measured e2e p95 375.8s -> ~71s and p99 581.6s -> ~118s (upper-bound recovery, assumes probes fully serial on critical path - true for the 1b chunk loop, partially true for concurrent per-card executor probes). To reach the claimed <0.1s/chunk floor, re-run db/create_neuract_ts_indexes.py to cover the 62 straggler gic_* tables + aux_hsd_plc_feedbacks (separate low-risk DDL op, not part of the 3-line code fix).
risk_notes: Overlap warnings: (1) any sibling finding proposing 'create the ::timestamptz indexes' (audit R2/F1 backlog) targets the SAME seconds - the indexes+knob already exist, this finding is the missing code-side half; do not sum. (2) Any TTL/cache finding on _VAL_CACHE (cache.resolution_ttl_s=120) reduces the FREQUENCY of the same 46,000s pool; do not sum with this cost reduction. (3) Same defect class survives in layer1b/basket/col_dict.py:39,61 and validate/data_load.py:40 (raw DATA_TS_CAST ORDER BY) - but obs shows that whole family is only ~4,921s/2d at 69ms avg, an order of magnitude smaller; fixing value_probe alone leaves it. (4) After this fix the pipeline tail is LLM-bound again (l2_emit ~19.8s/card per profiler baseline) - e2e p50 barely moves (8.1s median trace has cache-hit probes); this is a TAIL fix, not a median fix. Implementation risks: low - keep the fail-open/outage split intact; value_counts cache key is unchanged; if ts_index_fn knob is ever cleared the expression falls back to today's cast (behavior-identical). Concurrent cache-miss stampede (no lock on _VAL_CACHE) still duplicates probe work but becomes harmless at sub-second chunk cost.
verify_notes: Mechanism verified line-by-line: value_probe.py:73 orders by raw DATA_TS_CAST ('::timestamptz') while ems_exec/data/neuract.py _tsexpr() (lines 122-131) honors the neuract.ts_index_fn knob, which IS set to 'ts_imm' in cmd_catalog.app_config. neuract.ts_imm(t) is literally 'SELECT t::timestamptz' IMMUTABLE — so the proposed knob-aware ORDER BY picks the identical latest row (zero honesty/byte drift). Every headline number reproduced live: EXPLAIN ANALYZE on gic_27_n2_600_kva_ups_02_tm (277k rows) = 1,142ms Parallel Seq Scan/10,407 buffers vs 0.65ms ts_imm index scan vs 0.37ms plain-text index scan backward; obs_db_queries 2d window = 2,128 jsonb_each chunks, avg 21.6s, p95 29.1s, max 104,608ms (EXACT match to claimed 104.6s), ~46,000s total = 86% of ALL pipeline DB wall (next family only 4,921s); trace t_2aed84a5 = 8 un-staged chunks 184.2s + 3.85s asset_resolution single probe (matches 184.3s+3.9s claim); asset_resolution stage p95 = 189s (the ~200s 1b tail); e2e trace p95 375.8s -> 70.9s and p99 581.6s -> 117.8s with probe wall subtracted (571 traces / 205 probed). UNION ALL chunk shape verified to index-scan per covered branch. Index census: 302 gic tables, 296 plain-text-indexed (claim said 295 - essentially right), 240 ts_imm-indexed, 0 with neither. Constraints clean: generic data-layer 3-line change reusing config/neuract_dsn.ts_index_fn(), probe output identical, CMD_V2 untouched, no test pins the ORDER BY SQL text (checked test_regress_value_probe_ghosts.py / test_has_data_outage.py). Prefer the knob-aware ts_imm route over raw-text ORDER BY: mixed ISO-8601 text lengths (32 vs 35, audit F18) make a pure text sort mis-orderable on mixed-format tables.

title: neuract 'pool' is ONE shared psycopg2 connection - the entire executor DB leg serializes
evidence: _POOL dict keyed by (readonly, conn kwargs) holds exactly one connection for all threads; psycopg2 serializes concurrent cursors per connection. Measured raw tunnel RTT 16ms and index-backed reads EXPLAIN at <1ms server-side, yet the neuract door shows p50 49ms/query (102,239 queries/2d) rising to avg 72-108ms on busy traces = queueing. Trace t_0f16fca8: 416 exec queries = 29.8s summed on the single socket; per-card 75-149 queries, 5.5-6.5s summed each.
current_cost: ~18-30s of serialized exec-stage DB wall per DB-heavy 5-card page; 49-108ms per query vs ~20-25ms unqueued
scenario: 5-card panel page executor fill; /api/frame re-fetch; multi-asset lanes
proposal: Replace the singleton with a real checkout/checkin pool (mirror data/db_client._q_pool mechanics already proven for q()), size knob ~= layer2.emit_concurrency + 2. Keep the drop-on-error self-heal per connection.
est_saving: 5 cards x ~6s summed serialized ~= 25-30s exec DB wall -> (30s x 25/60 per-query speedup)/4 lanes ~= 3s: -15..-25s wall on DB-heavy exec pages, -3..-6s typical; frame refetch -1..-2s

title: value-probe cache keyed by frozenset(table-set) + 120s TTL - repeat prompts and multi-asset lanes re-pay full probes
evidence: _VAL_CACHE[frozenset(tables)] - overlapping sets share nothing, so a 3-feeder compare probing three overlapping table-sets re-scans shared tables per lane; TTL 120s means any prompt >2min later repays everything. Twin traces measured: cache-warm t_24da06f3 = 1.2s total DB vs cache-cold t_2aed84a5 = 206.8s.
current_cost: -20..-185s replayed every 120s window and per distinct table-set
scenario: repeat prompts >2min apart; RESPONSE_MULTI 3-feeder compares (worst 410-441s runs)
proposal: Cache per TABLE (table -> count entries), assemble any requested set from per-table hits and probe only misses. Interim config-only lever: raise cache.resolution_ttl_s (single UPDATE to app_config). Keep never-cache-empty semantics.
est_saving: With DB-1 unfixed: -20..-185s per cache-expired or per-lane replay; with DB-1 fixed still removes ~0.1-0.5s + RTT floors per re-probe and stops multi-asset overlap re-scans (-60..-185s on today's worst compare runs)

evidence: Both build ORDER BY "timestamp_utc"::timestamptz DESC from raw DATA_TS_CAST. Measured shape cost: 1,137.6ms on a 255k-row table (same plan as DB-1) vs 0.036-0.064ms indexed; obs shows these at 59-63ms on small tables in asset_resolution. Validate baseline max 119s is consistent with this sort on large tables.
current_cost: -0.1..-2.2s per 1b table sample; validate tail up to ~1.1s+ per large-table card read
scenario: 1b column basket on a 250k-row UPS table; validation stage tail (p50 312ms, max 119s)
proposal: Same fix as DB-1: route these two builders through the knob-aware ts expression (neuract.ts_imm(...)). 2 call sites.

title: window() issues 2 round trips and fans out per-member x per-column-set (N+1 over the 16ms tunnel)
evidence: Top-2 neuract shapes in obs 2d: first-row (ts_imm >= X ASC LIMIT 1) 17,463 calls + last-row (<= X DESC LIMIT 1) 17,463 calls, avg 72ms each, 2.51M ms combined. Trace t_0f16 card 5: 32 window halves over 8 member tables x 2 column-sets - windows are issued per member table of an aggregate card.
current_cost: 28-member aggregate card = 56+ round trips x ~50-72ms just for window baselines (~2.8-4s serialized)
scenario: 28-member panel-aggregate energy card; every windowed-delta leaf
proposal: (a) UNION ALL first+last rows in one statement (-1 RT per window call); (b) batch member tables as UNION ALL branches tagged by table name (-2N+1 RTs per aggregate leaf). Identical rows return; per-leaf honest degradation preserved (missing branch = None).
est_saving: 28-member card: 56 RTs x ~50ms -> 2 RTs x ~120ms = -2.5s per aggregate card; typical page (~72 window calls) -1.8s summed / -0.5..-1s wall

title: Per-table information_schema probes at ~78ms each (~35/run) vs one 35ms whole-schema fetch
evidence: obs 2d: 2,791 information_schema.columns probes in executor.card + 1,736 in layer2_card_ai.card, avg 70-75ms each (tunnel RTT + catalog-view join per call); trace t_0f16 paid 35 calls = 2.8s. Measured: bulk fetch of ALL neuract columns (302 tables) = 34.6ms in one query; single-table probe 14.2ms solo but 70-78ms under pipeline load. Cache TTL 120s per table forces steady re-probing.
current_cost: ~2.8s per cold 5-card page; ~4,500 tunnel round trips/2d
scenario: cold 5-card page (every card introspects its tables)
proposal: On first cache miss, prefetch the whole schema (table_name, column_name, data_type) into the shared TTL cache in one query; keep per-table never-cache-empty semantics. Optionally longer TTL for schema (columns change only on migrations).
est_saving: 35 x 78ms = 2.7s -> 1 x 35ms: -2..-3s per cold page; also removes contention pressure on the single conn (compounds with DB-3)

title: Per-leaf cmd_catalog policy/template reads - 24-25k repeats of constant keys; hot keys fixable config-only
evidence: obs 2d: data_quality_policy placeholder.scalar 24,059x, placeholder.narrative 23,746x, scrub.provenance_tokens 23,736x, scrub.* 8-9k each, reason_template 'unbound_by_emit' 25,029x - still live (20h: 2,947+1,739x2). Worst trace 18,175 catalog queries = 53s summed (13,704 inside layer2_card_ai.card). q() measures 0.044ms warm-solo but obs shows ~2.9ms avg under thread contention (pool lock + GIL). policy_read.txt() checks process-cached app_config FIRST and only falls through because these keys exist only in the legacy data_quality_policy table.
current_cost: ~10-13s summed on worst multi-card traces; ~0.5-2s typical page (contention); ~40k queries/day of load
scenario: multi-card pages with many blank/scrubbed leaves; notes stage (max 28.6s)
proposal: CONFIG-ONLY for the policy keys: INSERT the hot keys into app_config (canonical home) - policy_read then serves them from the process cache with zero code change. Add a small TTL/lru cache to config/reason_templates.reason() (~5 lines).

evidence: obs 2d: 1,857 latest_ts-shape calls in executor.card avg 108ms + 1,776 in layer2_card_ai.card avg 71.5ms; multi-card pages ask the same table's newest timestamp once per card.
current_cost: 8-10 calls x ~70-108ms per 5-card single-asset page = 0.6-1s

title: One full-window LAG scan per flag column; edge_count + bucketed_edges double-scan the same data
evidence: obs 2d: 4,552 edge_count + 2,216+1,345 bucketed_edges scans at avg 55-76ms. Each flag column of an events card triggers its own whole-window LAG pass; requesting both total and timeline scans identical rows twice.

title: Per-query db events unsampled: 51k synchronous telemetry events on worst multi-asset runs
stage: all stages (db_tap fires on every q()/neuract read)
evidence: Every DB query fires db_tap.record -> event build + jsonl write + pg enqueue. Measured 36.6us/query uncontended, 75.2us at 8-thread contention (bench in findings file). Worst execution trace_t_ed7e6dbe... = 51,372 events / 38.2MB one run; typical run 695 events. Median query is 4ms, so telemetry is ~1-2% per query and 90%+ of events are sub-10ms chatter.
current_cost: 51,372 x 75us = 3.9s on worst RESPONSE_MULTI (410-441s runs); 1,750 x 36.6us = 64ms on a 5-8-card panel page
proposal: Add obs.db.min_ms knob (record full kind=db events only for queries >= ~20-50ms or errors); per-stage span rollups (n_queries/rows_returned) already keep the counts. Cuts event volume 10-20x, also shrinks obs_db_queries (311k rows/133MB).
est_saving: -3.5s on worst multi-asset compares; -50ms on 5-8-card panel pages; obs_db_queries growth cut 10-20x
scenario: multi-asset compare / RESPONSE_MULTI tail

title: Replay capture encodes EVERY query's full result rows on the hot path
stage: executor + all query stages (replay hooks wrap q()/neuract/_run)
evidence: replay/hooks.db_q and db_rows call coding.encode(rows) on the querying thread for every query (recursive Python walk, datetime/Decimal tagging). Measured 6.2ms per 2,000-row query = 0.39us/cell. Worst run sql_r_cad41b51b5: 31,938 queries / 450,158 rows. Encoded rows + verbatim LLM bodies are held in Recorder.events RAM for the whole request (~100-150MB worst).
current_cost: 450k rows x 8 cells x 0.39us = ~1.4s worst run; ~18ms typical run; +100-150MB heap/GC pressure
proposal: replay.max_rows_per_event knob (e.g. 500; larger results stored as digest + n_rows + tape_partial marker so replay honestly TapeMisses to live) + replay.capture mode knob (on|errors_only|sample_1_in_N). No app_config replay.* rows exist today, so capture is unconditionally ON.
est_saving: -1.4s on worst multi-asset compares, -50ms on panel pages, -100MB+ heap (GC pauses unquantified)
scenario: multi-asset compare tail + every scenario's heap

title: jsonl sink does open/write/close per event under one global lock
file: obs/sink_jsonl.py:12-19 (also same pattern in obs/sql_trace.py, obs/failures.py, obs/stage.py, obs/ai_log.py)
evidence: sink_jsonl.write: json.dumps + os.makedirs + module-global threading.Lock + open()/write()/close() PER EVENT. Contended 8-thread benchmark doubles per-event cost (75.2 vs 36.6us) - the lock serializes L2 fan-out + executor pool threads. ~1,400 open/close cycles per typical run, ~100k on worst runs.
current_cost: ~15-20us/event of removable IO + serialization; part of the 3.9s worst-run total
proposal: Mirror sink_pg: bounded queue + daemon writer holding one open FD per active trace (close at trace end/idle). Keep drop-on-overflow + fail-open. Fold failures/sql_trace/pipeline_ writers into the same buffered writer.
est_saving: -1 to -2s on 51k-event worst runs; -25ms on panel pages; removes cross-thread telemetry serialization
scenario: multi-asset compare / concurrent runs

title: Every LLM call's full bodies captured FOUR times (three synchronously)
evidence: Per call: (a) ai_log urllib tap: json.loads(66.6KB request)+loads(response)+dumps+append = 189.7us, ai_<rid>.jsonl 52.5KB/line; (b) llm_tap -> trace jsonl 39KB/event, 158.7us; (c) replay hooks.llm records VERBATIM UNBOUNDED system/user into RAM then bundle; (d) obs_llm_calls (async). Worst run ai_r_92a2bfb0ae = 53.4MB/1,176 calls; disk totals ai_*=710MB, trace_*=610MB.
current_cost: 1,176 x ~0.5ms = 0.6s worst run; ~6ms typical; 1.3GB+ disk churn
proposal: Default the ai_log urllib tap OFF (V48_AI_LOG=0 knob; llm_tap+obs_llm_calls carry the same request/response/usage/params/decision - migrate payload_diff's ai leg to obs_llm_calls), and redact.bound the replay llm event's system/user (content_key hash computed before bounding, so tape lookup fidelity is unchanged).
est_saving: -0.6s on worst multi-asset runs; -710MB disk; -50MB worst-run heap
scenario: multi-asset compare / repeat-prompt sweeps

title: Trace lock acquired twice per event (attribute + next_seq)
evidence: Each event takes the ONE shared per-execution trace lock in span.attribute_db/attribute_ai AND again in event._base via trace.next_seq. 8-thread benchmark shows 2x per-event cost under contention (75.2 vs 36.6us) with 4-way L2 fan-out + executor pool all on one trace.
current_cost: ~half of the contention overhead on fan-out-heavy runs (~0.5-1s of the worst-run 3.9s)
proposal: Single lock acquisition per event (attribute_* returns the seq) or itertools.count() for seq (GIL-atomic). Pure telemetry refactor, no event-shape change.
est_saving: -0.5 to -1s on 51k-event worst runs; negligible at p50
